In one of the saddest parts of this epistle, Paul asks them to recall how sublimely happy they were when he first preached the gospel to them, and prays that all his efforts for them have not been in vain:

No man ever preached a Gospel sermon, except by the gift and power of
the Holy Ghost sent down from heaven. Without this power, there is no
light in the preaching. 8:138.

They stoned Paul (liyasantev ton paulon). First aorist active participle of liyazw, late verb from liyov for throwing stones (used by Paul referring to this one incident when alone he was stoned, #2Co 11:25). The wounds inflicted may have left some of the scars (stigmata) mentioned in #Ga 6:17. They stoned Paul as the chief speaker (Mercury) and passed by Barnabas (Jupiter). It was a Jewish mode of punishment as against Stephen and these Jews knew that Paul was the man that they had to deal…

Oxen and garlands (taurouv kai stemmata). Probably garlands to put on the oxen before they were slain. It was common to sacrifice bullocks to Jupiter and Mercury. (Robertson's NT Word Pictures) - garlands are put upon victims

Paul and Barnabas had now completed their mission and, if it had been feasible at the time, might have proceeded directly back to Syrian Antioch by way of the Cilician Gates through the Taurus Mountains. But they decided to return home by retracing their steps through the various cities where they had preached, those in Cyprus excepted. The two missionaries had doubtless given prayerful consideration to the over-all situation of their converts in the towns from whence they (the missionaries)…

They crossed from Perga to Attaleia, the port of Perga, sixteen miles down the Cestus, and capital of Pamphylia, to find a ship for Antioch in Syria. They came to Antioch in Pisidia. Pisidia was a province of Asia Minor, and was situated north of Pamphylia. Antioch was not in Pisidia, but within the limits of Phrygia; but it belonged to Pisidia, and was called Antioch of Pisidia to distinguish it from Antioch in Syria. (Barnes NT Notes)
